08-specs/distill-first-pass-spec.html.title: distill-first-pass-spec
08-specs/distill-first-pass-spec.html.description:
Distill is a minimal, terminal-native local AI harness for processing Obsidian notes after capture. Where pour is optimized for reflexive entry, distill is optimized for deliberate synthesis. It turns raw notes into reviewable knowledge artifacts without sacrificing source integrity, plaintext portability, or user authority.Core Philosophy: Synthesis After Capture: pour captures first. distill processes later. These responsibilities should stay separate. Source Integrity Above All: The original note is never silently rewritten or discarded. AI Isolation: Machine-generated output must live in explicit sections, sidecar files, or review buffers until accepted. Plaintext Is Still Forever: All durable state is stored as Markdown, YAML, or simple local metadata. No opaque hosted pipeline. Local-First Control: The model, prompts, embeddings, and note context should be operable on-device. distill is not a chatbot wrapped in a TUI. It is a controlled note-processing tool for Obsidian.The application has four primary execution paths:Running the base command opens the main processing queue. Header: Model/provider status, vault readiness, pending review count. Body: Notes ready for processing or review. Sidebar or Tabs: Filters such as inbox, pending, applied, failed. Quick Actions: Start processing, open review, skip, re-run. The queue should feel operational and calm, more like a triage board than a chat session.Bypasses the queue and processes one note directly. snapshots source state runs configured AI passes opens the review surface rather than applying edits automatically This is the equivalent of pour <module>: a direct path for focused work.Opens the pending suggestion workflow.The review view is the core product surface. It should support rapid tabbing through proposal types: summary links entities questions metadata rewrite The left side remains canonical source. The right side shows structured proposals. Acceptance is explicit and granular.The following commands are in scope as first-class routes: distill inbox distill note <path> distill review distill links <path> distill apply These should map to stable internal workflows rather than becoming arbitrary prompt entry points.
distill must inherit guidance from The Pour Manifesto without copying pour's exact interaction model.The manifesto gives four durable constraints: Capture and synthesis are separate acts. pour handles the raw moment. distill handles the slower second pass. Plaintext output matters. AI assistance should not move note processing into a black box or proprietary state store. The tool must get out of the way. distill should reduce note-maintenance friction, not create a sprawling "AI workspace." The command is the ritual. distill should feel like an intentional refinement pass over notes, not a general-purpose assistant shell. The practical implication is: distill must be review-first, bounded, and file-native.Each note should move through a predictable local pipeline: discover candidate note snapshot original state normalize markdown for model input gather vault context run one or more specialized AI passes store proposals in an isolated state representation review proposals in the TUI apply accepted changes back to the note This should be modular. A failed links pass should not invalidate an otherwise successful summary pass.distill should avoid a single giant "improve this note" prompt. The minimum useful passes are: Summary Pass: compress the note into a durable, reviewable synopsis Link Pass: propose [[wikilinks]] to existing vault notes Entity Pass: identify people, places, topics, projects, media, or gear worth linking Metadata Pass: propose tags, aliases, and lightweight structured fields Question Pass: identify unresolved threads or follow-up prompts Optional later pass: Rewrite Pass: propose a cleaner version of the note body without replacing the source by default This is the most important architectural rule.The original note remains canonical until the user applies accepted changes. AI output must be isolated in one of the following ways: explicit note sections such as ## AI Summary or ## Proposed Links sibling sidecar files such as <note>.distill.md local state/cache files outside the vault for intermediate review data Preferred first-pass model: source note remains untouched during processing proposals are written to a sibling .distill.md file or equivalent local state accepted changes are merged back into the note through an explicit apply step This provides: auditability rollback safety clean separation between source and machine output compatibility with Obsidian sync and version control The link pass should consult vault-local context rather than hallucinating note structure.Useful context sources: note titles aliases tags frontmatter metadata nearby folder structure optional embeddings/vector index distill should prefer linking to existing notes over inventing new structure unless explicitly configured to suggest new note creation.The model layer should be provider-agnostic.Examples: local HTTP model server llama.cpp ollama custom local inference bridge The spec should treat model access as a pluggable provider, but all core flows must assume the provider may be slow, imperfect, or occasionally offline.The first-pass TUI should be built around three core screens: Queue: candidate notes, status, and pending work Review: source note vs. AI proposals Apply Summary: accepted operations and resulting file changes The review surface should optimize for fast, repetitive approval work.Required characteristics: source always visible proposals grouped by type tabbing between proposal groups is fast and predictable accept/reject/edit actions are explicit no hidden background mutation of canonical notes Suggested keys: Tab / Shift+Tab to move between proposal panes j / k or arrows to move items a accept r reject e edit A apply all accepted q quit The interface should feel technical, sparse, and trustworthy.Prefer: pending accepted applied source proposal links Avoid: conversational assistant language celebratory AI wording anything that implies the model is authoritative The TUI should read like a careful processing tool, not an AI companion.The source note is the authority.distill may: append accepted summaries insert accepted wikilinks add accepted metadata write derived sidecar files distill must not: silently replace the original body reorder note structure without review delete original content by default The first-pass application layer should support these mutation classes: insert or replace an AI Summary section insert accepted wikilinks into note body or footer block add frontmatter fields such as distill_status, distill_last_run, or tags create a related note stub when explicitly approved Each applied change should be narrow and inspectable.Useful local metadata fields may include: distill_status distill_last_run distill_model distill_reviewed These should remain optional and human-readable.The following are explicitly in scope for v0.1: queue view for candidate notes single-note processing path local provider abstraction for AI execution source snapshotting / persistence isolated proposal storage review UI with proposal tabs link suggestions against existing vault context summary and metadata passes explicit apply step for accepted changes The following are explicitly deferred: autonomous background processing with silent application chat-style prompting inside the main interface aggressive note rewriting as a default workflow multi-user collaboration cloud-only model dependency fully automatic graph restructuring of the vault If pour is the reflex that gets life into the vault, distill is the quiet second pass that makes the vault more legible.distill should help the user: preserve original notes isolate AI output strengthen wiki linking tab through suggestions quickly apply synthesis without surrendering authorship That is the correct complement to the manifesto: capture first, synthesize later, and keep both acts native to plaintext and the terminal.

08-specs/pour-design-spec.html.title: pour-design-spec
08-specs/pour-design-spec.html.description:
Pour is a blazing-fast, terminal-native (TUI) capture tool designed to eliminate the friction of logging structured data into Obsidian. Built in Rust (using ratatui), it acts as a headless data-entry client, allowing users to rapidly "pour" thoughts and coffee logs directly into their vault without breaking their CLI workflow.Core Philosophy: Offline-First Resilience: Works with the Obsidian Local REST API, but falls back seamlessly to direct file-system operations if the vault is closed. Snappy & Rhythmic: Designed around the pour command. Fast muscle memory. Data Integrity: Generates strict YAML frontmatter and standard Markdown to ensure 100% compatibility with Obsidian Properties (Bases) and Dataview. The application has two primary execution paths:Running the base command opens the main interactive hub. Header: Displays vault connection status (🟢 API Connected | 🟡 Direct File Mode). Body: Shows a summary of today's stats (e.g., "Poured today: 2 Coffees, 1 Journal"). [Deviation: not implemented in v1 — dashboard shows module list with connection status only.] Menu: Navigable list to launch specific modules (me, coffee). Bypasses the dashboard and launches directly into a specific data-entry view. pour me — Opens the journal appending view. pour coffee — Opens the coffee logging form. Upon submitting a form, the app does not immediately exit. It transitions to a Summary View displaying: A success message with the destination file path. Options: [Enter] Main Menu, [A] Pour Another → .., [Q] Quit, [O] Open file in $EDITOR. [Deviation: [O] not implemented in v1 — summary supports Enter, A, and Q only.] Pour uses a dual-pronged approach to writing data:
Primary (API): Attempts a fast local HTTPS request via reqwest to the Obsidian Local REST API (https://127.0.0.1:27124, accepts self-signed certs). [Deviation: originally spec'd as HTTP; implementation uses HTTPS with danger_accept_invalid_certs.] Fallback (File System): If the connection is refused, it gracefully falls back to std::fs to write directly to the absolute vault path defined in the configuration. API Authentication: The REST API plugin requires a Bearer token. Pour supports two sources: api_key field under [vault] in config.toml. POUR_API_KEY environment variable (takes precedence over config if set). 3-tier fallback (API → disk scan → cache → freetext) with async background refresh.When allow_create = true on a dynamic_select field, the dropdown enters search mode as the user types — filtering options by case-insensitive substring match and showing a "Create new" affordance for unmatched text. Submitting a novel value auto-creates a bare note at {source}/{sanitized_value}.md (via the transport layer) before the module output is written. The new entry is appended to the in-memory cache immediately. [Deviation: the original spec described dynamic_select as a closed list only; inline creation was added post-spec.]When a dynamic_select field has create_template referencing a [templates.<name>] section, novel values trigger a sub-form overlay instead of bare stub creation. The overlay prompts the user for template-defined fields (text, number, static_select), then writes a note with full YAML frontmatter. [Deviation: not in original spec. Added to support richer inline-created notes without leaving the TUI.]An optional post_create_command fires an Obsidian plugin command (e.g. templater:run) via the REST API's /commands/ endpoint after note creation. This bridges Pour's structured data capture with Obsidian's plugin ecosystem — Pour handles frontmatter, the plugin handles body/presentation. The command is best-effort: silently skipped on filesystem transport. [Deviation: command execution via REST API was not in original spec.]Append vs. create modes, and how fields map to frontmatter/body.When wikilink = true on a text, static_select, or dynamic_select field, the output value is wrapped in Obsidian wikilink syntax ([[value]]) before being written to frontmatter. This creates graph edges between the current note and the named note. For comma-separated multi-values, each item is wrapped individually. [Deviation: wikilink wrapping was not in the original field spec; added alongside inline creation.]Full TOML schema, field type reference, and validation rules — see config schema section. Language: Rust (2024 Edition)
TUI Framework: ratatui + crossterm
Serialization: serde, serde_json, toml, toml_edit [Deviation: serde_yaml was originally included but removed — YAML frontmatter uses custom serialization instead. See ADR-002-Custom-YAML-Serialization.]
Network: reqwest (with tokio for async fetching)
Time: chrono (for file formatting and timestamps) The following are explicitly in scope for v0.1: Dashboard with connection status and module menu pour me (append mode with Templater integration + atomic note fallback) pour coffee (create mode with frontmatter generation) Hybrid transport layer (API → filesystem fallback) Dynamic data fetching (API → disk scan → cache → freetext) Configurable append templates with {{callout}} placeholder resolved from module-level callout_type; field-level callout wraps textarea body output in > [!type] blockquote syntax allow_create on dynamic_select fields — inline creation with freetext filtering, "Create new" affordance, and auto-created bare notes wikilink on text, static_select, and dynamic_select fields — wraps output in [[...]] for Obsidian graph connectivity Configurable theme (accent color, border style) [Deviation: not implemented in v1 — all styling is inline via ratatui's Style builder.] Post-execution summary view required field validation The following are explicitly in scope for v0.2: Template-driven inline creation with sub-form overlay (create_template + [templates]) Post-creation command hook (post_create_command) for Obsidian plugin integration Command execution via REST API transport (/commands/{commandId}/) The following are explicitly deferred: pour music module (generic config supports it when ready) Rich validation (min/max, regex) Tag-based dynamic_select sources Plugin/extension system Nested templates / recursive sub-forms Dynamic data sources in template fields (only static_select, not dynamic_select) TUI configure screen support for create_template / post_create_command fields

01-concepts/inline-note-creation.html.title: Inline-Note-Creation
01-concepts/inline-note-creation.html.description:
When a dynamic_select field has allow_create = true, Pour extends the capture flow to include vault-side note creation. This closes the loop between data entry and data management — the user never leaves the TUI to scaffold a new option. The user types a value that doesn't match any existing option in the dropdown. The dropdown shows + Create "{value}" as a visual affordance. On form submit, Pour detects the novel value and auto-creates a bare note at {source}/{value}.md with minimal frontmatter (date only). The note is created before the main module output is written, so any wikilink = true references resolve immediately in Obsidian. The local cache is updated so subsequent form loads include the new option without a transport round-trip. This feature extends The-3-Tier-Data-Fallback with an output side. The fallback pipeline handles reading options into the TUI; inline creation handles writing new options back to the vault. Together they form a closed loop:Vault ──[fetch]──> Cache ──> Dropdown ──[novel value]──> Auto-create ──> Vault When all three tiers fail and the dropdown is empty, allow_create fields accept freetext input (Tier 3 behavior). The submitted value triggers note creation, seeding the cache for future sessions.User-typed values are sanitized before becoming filenames: Characters invalid in cross-platform filenames (: ? * < > | " \ /) are replaced with - Consecutive dashes are collapsed Leading/trailing dashes and whitespace are trimmed Windows reserved device names (CON, NUL, COM1-COM9, LPT1-LPT9) are rejected Empty results after sanitization are skipped Duplicate detection is case-insensitive and checks both the raw typed value and its sanitized form against existing options.Note creation is best-effort. If the transport layer fails (network error, permission issue), the main form write still proceeds. The failure is logged to stderr but does not block the user's capture flow.By default, inline creation produces a bare stub — a note with only a date field. For richer notes, add a create_template reference to the field:[[modules.coffee.fields]] name = "bean" field_type = "dynamic_select" prompt = "Bean" source = "Coffee/Beans" allow_create = true wikilink = true create_template = "bean" post_create_command = "templater:run" # optional — fires an Obsidian command after creation When the user types a novel value, a sub-form overlay appears with the template's fields. The user fills them in without leaving Pour, and the created note gets full structured frontmatter.The overlay is a centered modal that appears over the main form: Shows the template's fields (text, number, static_select) Tab/Shift+Tab navigates fields Enter on the submit button creates the note Esc cancels without creating anything Falls back to bare stub creation if the terminal is too small post_create_command fires an Obsidian command via the REST API after note creation. This bridges Pour's data capture with Obsidian plugins: Pour writes frontmatter (structured data it collected) Templater (or any plugin) adds body content, formatting, and dynamic expressions Only fires when connected via API; silently skipped on filesystem transport Templates are defined at the top level of config.toml:[templates.bean] path = "Coffee/Beans/{{name}}.md" [[templates.bean.fields]] name = "roaster" field_type = "text" prompt = "Roaster" [[templates.bean.fields]] name = "origin" field_type = "static_select" prompt = "Origin" options = ["Ethiopia", "Colombia", "Kenya"]
{{name}} is replaced with the user's typed value. The path also supports strftime tokens (%Y, %m, %d). See field-types > Templates for the full schema reference.# Basic — bare stub creation [[modules.coffee.fields]] name = "bean" field_type = "dynamic_select" prompt = "Bean" source = "Coffee/Beans" allow_create = true wikilink = true # Advanced — template-driven creation with Templater hook [[modules.coffee.fields]] name = "bean" field_type = "dynamic_select" prompt = "Bean" source = "Coffee/Beans" allow_create = true wikilink = true create_template = "bean" post_create_command = "templater:run"
See field-types for the full allow_create, wikilink, create_template, and post_create_command reference.

04-architecture/adr/adr-004-api-append-r.title: ADR-004-API-Append-Read-Modify-Write
04-architecture/adr/adr-004-api-append-r.description:
Date: 2026-04-02
Status: AcceptedContext:
The Obsidian Local REST API's heading-targeted PATCH append (Operation: append, Target-Type: heading) inserts a *** thematic break between the existing section content and the appended content. This is a server-side behavior that cannot be suppressed via headers or body formatting. The result is visible clutter in daily notes that accumulates with every pour me entry.The previous implementation used two requests: a GET for the document map (to resolve the full :: delimited heading path) followed by the PATCH append.Decision:
Replace the heading-targeted PATCH with a read-modify-write cycle: GET the file as text/markdown, splice the new content under the target heading in-memory (using the same insertion logic as the filesystem transport), then PUT the full file back.Consequences:
Positive: Eliminates the unwanted *** separator. Append output is now identical regardless of whether the API or filesystem transport is used. Also removes the document-map resolution step, simplifying the code.
Negative: Introduces a read-modify-write race — if another process writes to the same file between the GET and PUT, that write is lost. This is acceptable because pour is a single-user CLI tool and concurrent writes to the same heading are not a realistic scenario.
Neutral: Request count stays at two (was GET map + PATCH, now GET file + PUT file). No measurable performance difference.

05-notes/inline-creation-future-phases.h.title: inline-creation-future-phases
05-notes/inline-creation-future-phases.h.description:
[templates.<name>] config sections define reusable note templates with path (supporting {{name}} and strftime), and fields (restricted to text, number, static_select). Referenced by create_template on dynamic_select fields. Validation enforces template existence, {{name}} in path, no .. traversal, unique field names, and reserved name (date, name) rejection.Files: src/config.rs, src/autocreate.rs, tests/config.rs, tests/autocreate.rsWhen allow_create + create_template is set and the user enters a novel value, a centered modal sub-form overlay appears with the template's fields. Supports text input with char-index cursor, number validation, static_select dropdowns. Submit creates the note with full frontmatter, Esc cancels. Graceful degradation to bare stub if terminal too small.Files: src/tui/form.rs, src/app.rspost_create_command fires an Obsidian command via REST API /commands/{commandId}/ after template-driven note creation. Enables coordination with Templater and other plugins. Silently skipped on filesystem transport.Files: src/transport/api.rs, src/transport/mod.rs, src/main.rs
Nested templates / recursive sub-forms
Dynamic data sources in template fields (only static_select supported, not dynamic_select)
Template inheritance or composition
TUI configure screen support for editing create_template / post_create_command fields
Dataview DQL query sources for template field options

info_showOrphanNodes.description: Show nodes that are not connected to any other nodes.

info_showAttachments.description: Show attachments like images and PDFs as nodes in the graph.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.
